verses.fhb_text: There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.
verses.decision_notes: Standard Pauline opening; "Christ Jesus" preserved per FHB-1.2-20 Greek convention.

verses.fhb_text: For the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus has set you free from the law of sin and death.
verses.decision_notes: "Spirit of life" preserved as Paul's chosen relational phrasing — Spirit named under his life-giving role.

verses.fhb_text: For what the law could not do, weakened as it was by the flesh, our Father did: by sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh and as a sin offering, he condemned sin in the flesh,
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Theos resolves to our Father — Father sends the Son, classic distinguishing marker.

Renderings:
• "our Father did" — Theos distinguished from Son per FHB-4.3.2-1 (Father sending Son)
• "his own Son" — preserve possessive; Father's Person-relation to the Son

verses.fhb_text: so that the righteous requirement of the law might be fulfilled in us, who walk not according to the flesh but according to the Spirit.
verses.decision_notes: Continues v.3 purpose clause; Spirit retained as Paul names him.

verses.fhb_text: For those who live according to the flesh set their minds on the things of the flesh, but those who live according to the Spirit set their minds on the things of the Spirit.
verses.decision_notes: Paul's flesh/Spirit contrast preserved.

verses.fhb_text: For the mind of the flesh is death, but the mind of the Spirit is life and peace.
verses.decision_notes: Aphorism preserved in parallel structure.

verses.fhb_text: For the mind of the flesh is hostile to our Father, for it does not submit to our Father's law, indeed it cannot.
verses.decision_notes: Theos twice — Father is the one whose law the flesh resists; continues v.3 distinction from the Son.

verses.fhb_text: Those who are in the flesh cannot please our Father.
verses.decision_notes: Theos → our Father per chapter's Father-distinguished frame.

verses.fhb_text: But you are not in the flesh, but in the Spirit, if indeed the Spirit of our Father dwells in you. Anyone who does not have the Spirit of Christ does not belong to him.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Paul's deliberate Father/Christ Spirit-chiasm preserved per FHB-1.2-7 D.

Renderings:
• "Spirit of our Father" — pneuma Theou with Father distinguished from Christ in the next clause
• "Spirit of Christ" — preserved as Paul writes it

Greek: pneuma Theou ... pneuma Christou → the same Spirit named under two relational angles, not two Spirits.

verses.fhb_text: But if Christ is in you, although the body is dead because of sin, the Spirit is life because of righteousness.
verses.decision_notes: Christ indwelling preserved in apposition with Spirit indwelling from v.9 — Paul's trinitarian intimacy.

verses.fhb_text: And if the Spirit of our Father, who raised Jesus from the dead, dwells in you, then he who raised Christ from the dead will also give life to your mortal bodies through his Spirit who dwells in you.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: "the Spirit of him who raised Jesus" resolves to our Father per chapter's Father-Son distinction.

Renderings:
• "the Spirit of our Father, who raised Jesus" — descriptive participle made explicit; the resurrector is the Father (Acts 2:24)
• "he who raised Christ" — preserve Paul's descriptive form on second occurrence (path continuity)

verses.fhb_text: So then, brothers and sisters, we are debtors — not to the flesh, to live according to the flesh.
verses.decision_notes: Em dash earns its place — sharp parenthetical break Paul uses for emphasis.

verses.fhb_text: For if you live according to the flesh, you will die; but if by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body, you will live.
verses.decision_notes: Parallel clauses joined with semicolon per modern English rhythm.

verses.fhb_text: For all who are led by the Spirit of our Father are children of our Father.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Direct filial context — huioi Theou rendered with full Father-relational naming.

Renderings:
• "Spirit of our Father" — pneumati Theou per FHB-1.2-7
• "children of our Father" — huioi Theou with explicit filial frame per FHB-5.3.1-1

verses.fhb_text: For you did not receive a spirit of slavery to fall back into fear, but you received the Spirit of adoption, by whom we cry, "Abba! Father!"
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Abba preserved as scripture writes it — the Aramaic intimate Father-word taken up from Jesus's own prayer (Mark 14:36).

Renderings:
• "Spirit of adoption" — pneuma huiothesias preserved; FHB-1.2-7 Spirit-of-adoption relational name
• "Abba! Father!" — Aramaic loanword preserved per FHB-1.2-26

Greek: abba ho patēr — Paul keeps the Aramaic word the Roman church learned from Jesus's own prayer.
verses.footnote: Abba is the Aramaic word for Father — the intimate word Jesus himself prayed in Gethsemane. By the Spirit of adoption, the same word becomes ours: we cry to our Father with the same closeness Jesus does.

verses.fhb_text: The Spirit himself bears witness with our spirit that we are children of our Father.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Spirit's inner-witness of our Father's adoption — climactic naming.

Renderings:
• "children of our Father" — tekna Theou with explicit filial context flowing from v.15 Abba cry

verses.fhb_text: And if children, then heirs — heirs of our Father and co-heirs with Christ, provided we suffer with him in order that we may also be glorified with him.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Inheritance language — fully filial.

Renderings:
• "heirs of our Father" — klēronomoi Theou; inheritance context per FHB-4.1.2-1 / FHB-5.3.1-1 triggers explicit Father naming
• Em dash carries the climactic apposition ("then heirs — heirs of our Father...")

verses.fhb_text: For I consider that the sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory that is to be revealed to us.
verses.decision_notes: Modern English phrasing; preserves Paul's eschatological hope without archaism.

verses.fhb_text: For the creation waits with eager longing for the revealing of the children of our Father.
verses.decision_notes: huiōn tou Theou — sons/children of God in clearly filial-eschatological context → "children of our Father."

verses.fhb_text: For the creation was subjected to futility — not willingly, but because of him who subjected it — in hope
verses.decision_notes: Em dash pair carries Paul's parenthetical aside; "him who subjected it" preserved as Paul's descriptive form (the Father in view but not named directly).

verses.fhb_text: that the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to decay and obtain the freedom of the glory of the children of our Father.
verses.decision_notes: Second tekna Theou in the passage; same Father-rendering carries through.

verses.fhb_text: For we know that the whole creation has been groaning together in the pains of childbirth until now.
verses.decision_notes: Paul's birth-pang metaphor preserved.

verses.fhb_text: And not only the creation, but we ourselves — who have the firstfruits of the Spirit — also groan inwardly as we wait eagerly for adoption, the redemption of our bodies.
verses.decision_notes: Em dash pair carries the parenthetical "who have the firstfruits of the Spirit"; huiothesia preserved as "adoption."

verses.fhb_text: For in this hope we were saved. Now hope that is seen is not hope, for who hopes for what he sees?
verses.decision_notes: Paul's hope-aphorism preserved in clean English.

verses.fhb_text: But if we hope for what we do not see, we wait for it with patience.
verses.decision_notes: Continues v.24 aphorism.

verses.fhb_text: Likewise the Spirit also helps us in our weakness. For we do not know what to pray for as we ought, but the Spirit himself intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Spirit's intercession — preserved with masculine personal pronoun per FHB-7.3.6-1.

Renderings:
• "the Spirit himself" — auto to pneuma; personal-Person language preserved

verses.fhb_text: And he who searches hearts knows what is the mind of the Spirit, because the Spirit intercedes for the saints according to our Father's will.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The heart-searcher and the will named are the Father.

Renderings:
• "he who searches hearts" — descriptive form preserved; Father in view
• "according to our Father's will" — kata Theon resolves to the Father per chapter context (Spirit interceding to the Father)

verses.fhb_text: And we know that for those who love our Father, all things work together for good — for those who are called according to his purpose.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Theos here — relational love-of-God context → our Father.

Renderings:
• "those who love our Father" — tois agapōsin ton Theon; love-of-God is filial in Paul's grammar
• Em dash carries the appositional clarification ("for those who are called according to his purpose")

verses.fhb_text: For those whom he foreknew, he also predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son, that he might be the firstborn among many brothers and sisters.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Father's eternal plan — Jesus as firstborn Son, believers as conformed-image siblings.

Renderings:
• "image of his Son" — eikona tou huiou autou preserved; Father's Person-relation to the Son
• "firstborn among many brothers and sisters" — prōtotokon en pollois adelphois; inclusive English idiom for brothers + sisters together
verses.footnote: Our Father's eternal purpose is a family — Jesus the firstborn Son, and many brothers and sisters conformed to his likeness. Adoption is not metaphor; it is the family our Father always meant to have.

verses.fhb_text: And those whom he predestined he also called, and those whom he called he also justified, and those whom he justified he also glorified.
verses.decision_notes: Paul's golden chain preserved with parallel structure intact.

verses.fhb_text: What then shall we say to these things? If our Father is for us, who can be against us?
verses.decision_notes: Theos → our Father; the famous question reads as the family-confidence it always was.

verses.fhb_text: He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all — how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Akedah echo — the Father gave what Abraham was spared from giving.

Renderings:
• "he who did not spare his own Son" — descriptive form preserved; the Father is the subject Paul names without naming
• Em dash carries the rhetorical question's pivot

verses.fhb_text: Who shall bring any charge against our Father's chosen ones? Our Father is the one who justifies.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Two Theos references, both Father (chosen ones belong to him; he justifies them).

Renderings:
• "our Father's chosen ones" — eklektōn Theou per NT X-of-God general clause
• "Our Father is the one who justifies" — Theos ho dikaiōn; Father-as-justifier is Paul's standard frame

verses.fhb_text: Who shall condemn? Christ Jesus is the one who died — more than that, who was raised — who is at the right hand of our Father, who indeed is interceding for us.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Right hand of God → our Father per FHB-1.2-1 NT X-of-God general clause.

Renderings:
• "right hand of our Father" — en dexia tou Theou; Father distinguished from Son in the same verse
• Em dash pair carries Paul's resurrection-emphasis aside

verses.fhb_text: Who shall separate us from the love of Christ? Tribulation, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or danger, or sword?
verses.decision_notes: Love of Christ preserved; Christ is the explicit antecedent.

verses.fhb_text: As it is written, "For your sake we are being killed all day long; we are regarded as sheep to be slaughtered."
verses.decision_notes: Paul quoting Ps 44:22; preserve OT psalm voice as written.

verses.fhb_text: No, in all these things we are more than conquerors through him who loved us.
verses.decision_notes: "Him who loved us" — Christ in view from v.35 antecedent; preserve Paul's descriptive form.

verses.fhb_text: For I am convinced that neither death nor life, neither angels nor rulers, neither things present nor things to come, nor powers,
verses.decision_notes: Paul's catalog preserved with paired conjunctions for English rhythm.

verses.fhb_text: neither height nor depth, nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of our Father in Christ Jesus our Lord.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Climactic — agape tou Theou tēs en Christō Iēsou. Father distinguished from Son in the same phrase; "our Lord" preserved per FHB-1.2-20 doxological close.

Renderings:
• "love of our Father" — agape tou Theou per NT X-of-God general clause
• "in Christ Jesus our Lord" — Christological mediation preserved with confessional weight

chapter_notes: Romans 8 is the canonical adoption chapter — the most concentrated Father-Son-Spirit passage in Paul. Theos throughout the chapter is consistently the Father, distinguished from Christ by sending (v.3), raising Jesus (v.11), Spirit-of-God / Spirit-of-Christ chiasm (v.9), seat at right hand (v.34), love mediated through Christ (v.39). The Spirit of adoption (v.15) crying "Abba, Father" is THE chapter's anchor — every Theos reading aligns with the Father whom we cry to. Two new footnote categories established for the canon: abba_father (v.15) and firstborn_among_brothers (v.29). v.32 Akedah echo handled via body translation without footnote (lamb_of_father category already used). Trinitarian distinctions are preserved exactly as Paul writes them — Father, Son, Holy Spirit named under their relational angles without collapse.
